explanation:
## How Water Tank Capacity Is Calculated

Residential water tank capacity is determined by occupant count and desired backup supply duration. This calculator applies the formula with your inputs, checks reasonable ranges, and alerts you to unusual values.

## Key Factors

- **Daily consumption**: 200 liters per person is the standard allocation
- **Reserve days**: 1 day for urban areas with reliable supply; 2–3 days for suburban or rural properties
- **Tank material**: High-density polyethylene (triple-wall) is most common and durable
- **Installation**: Always on a solid, level base. Never directly on soft soil
- **Water pressure**: Tank positioned 8–10 meters above fixtures ensures adequate pressure

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (building permits, system design, code compliance), consult a licensed plumber or engineer. Values are current to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How much water does one person use per day?
faq.a: The standard is 200 liters (53 gallons) per person per day. Low-consumption homes: 150 L. High-use urban homes: 250 L.

faq.q: How many days of backup water supply do I need?
faq.a: 1 day for urban areas with reliable municipal supply. 2–3 days for suburban or rural properties.

faq.q: What material should my water tank be made of?
faq.a: High-density polyethylene (triple-wall) is most common and durable. Fiberglass is an alternative. Avoid older asbestos-cement tanks.

faq.q: Where should I place my water tank?
faq.a: Always on a solid, level base or platform. Never directly on soft soil. Ensure the base can support the full weight when filled.

faq.q: How do I get good water pressure?
faq.a: Position your tank 8–10 meters (26–33 feet) above your fixtures. This creates adequate pressure of 3–5 bar (45–70 psi).
Yes. All calculators on Hacé Cuentas are free and require no registration.
